source: lastreetnames.com by Mark Tapio Kines (2,100+ entries)
purpose: LA street name origins for scavenger hunt clue generation

streets.who: French-Canadian businessman, 9th mayor of Los Angeles (1874)
facts: Only Frenchman to ever serve as mayor of Los Angeles
Built reservoirs, laid gas lines, planted 4,000 street trees
Came to LA in 1852
His brother Victor also served on the City Council
Beaudry Avenue runs directly into Victor Heights — the residential neighborhood named for brother Victor. The brothers' legacies are literally connected by the street grid.
Michael's favorite LA street fact: 'What LA street named after an early LA resident runs into a residential neighborhood named for that resident's brother?'

streets.who: Mexican governor of Alta California (1836-1842)
facts: Led a coup at age 27 to seize the governorship
His era was marked by armed skirmishes between NorCal and SoCal
The instability rendered California vulnerable to U.S. takeover in 1846
Quit politics in 1848, died in obscurity

streets.who: Named to co-opt the Beverly name for property values
facts: Created by combining sections of 1st Street and Temple Street in 1916
Until 1934, the winding stretch of what is now Sunset Blvd between Benedict Canyon and the coast was actually called Beverly Boulevard
Didn't fully open until 1926

streets.who: Renamed by printer Fred Lind Alles (1851-1945)
facts: Originally 'Fort Street' after Fort Moore, built by American soldiers after Mexican-American War
Renamed Feb 1890 because German immigrants confused 'Fort Street' with 'Fourth Street'
Fred Lind Alles spent months petitioning for the change
Several other streets (Eternity Street, Buena Vista Street, Downey Avenue) were later absorbed into Broadway

streets.who: Indigenous name from the Kizh (Tongva) people
facts: Follows an ancient footpath — the Cahuenga Pass — between the LA basin and the village near present-day Universal City
Treaty of Cahuenga signed here Jan 13, 1847 by Andres Pico and John C. Fremont, ending California's involvement in the Mexican-American War
Possible meanings: 'place of the hill' or 'place of the fox'

streets.who: Labor leader, co-founded United Farm Workers
facts: Co-founded UFW with Dolores Huerta and Gilbert Padilla in 1962
Thoroughfare consists of former Brooklyn Avenue, Macy Street, and a portion of Sunset Boulevard
Renamed in his honor in 1994, a year after his death

streets.namedAfter: Echo Park — echoes heard around the reservoir
streets.who: Named by city parks superintendent Joseph Henry Tomlinson
facts: LA's primary water source was its river; a reservoir was dug here in 1868 (Reservoir Site No. 4 — today's Echo Park Lake)
Real estate speculator Thomas Kelley donated 33 acres for parkland in 1891
The name 'Echo Park' was coined for the echoes Tomlinson heard around the reservoir

streets.namedAfter: General Jose Figueroa (1792-1835), governor of Alta California
streets.who: Governor who secularized the missions and began offering land grants
facts: First laid out in 1849 as Calle de los Chapules — 'Grasshopper Street'
Legend: if grasshoppers made it past this road, the grape harvest would be doomed
Renamed Pearl Street in 1874, then Figueroa in 1897
The old Figueroa (a few blocks west) was renamed Boylston

streets.who: Named on 1849 Ord/Hutton survey map
facts: Part of alleged 'Faith, Hope, and Charity' trio of downtown streets — but no evidence Flower was ever called 'Faith Street'
Merchant Harris Newmark claimed Emeline Childs named all three in the 1860s
Its only name has always been Flower

streets.who: Property owners petitioned because they hated 'living on charity' jokes
facts: Originally Charity Street on the 1849 map
LA Daily Times: Council 'took pity on the people who were sick of living on charity'
Residents initially suggested Pacific Avenue, but that name was taken

streets.who: Welsh mining magnate who donated 3,015 acres for a public park
facts: Donated land as Christmas gift in 1896, requesting it be for 'the plain people'
In 1903, shot out his wife Tina's right eye during a drunken rampage at Santa Monica's Hotel Arcadia
Spent two years in San Quentin
After his death, city used his money to build Griffith Observatory and the Greek Theatre
Boulevard was originally Childs Avenue

streets.who: Harvey and Daeida Wilcox branded 120 acres 'Hollywood' in 1887
facts: Originally called Prospect Avenue, renamed 1901
Competing origins: (1) woman on train told Ida about 'Hollywood' near Chicago; (2) sister claimed Ida named it after California holly; (3) landowner Ivar Weid suggested it
Hollywood annexed by LA in 1910

streets.who: Father Dr. Leonce Hoover pioneered LA's wine industry
facts: Was once the western boundary of the city of LA
Briefly renamed 'West Boundary Street' in 1892, but it didn't stick
Streets west of Hoover run on strict north-south grid; east of Hoover, at 36-degree angle

streets.namedAfter: Possibly Dr. Alexander W. Hope (c. 1820-1856) or the virtue of hope
streets.who: Virginia physician who became LA's first chief of police in 1851
facts: On the 1849 Ord/Hutton survey map, though the street didn't exist until late 1860s
Spanish name: Calle de las Esperanzas
Theory: part of 'Faith, Hope, and Charity' trio with Flower and Grand

streets.namedAfter: Hyperion — Greek Titan, father of Helios (sun god), or Longfellow's novel
streets.who: Laid out in 1887 on the Childs Heights tract
facts: Either named for Greek Titan (father of Helios, god of the sun) or Longfellow's novel Hyperion (1836)
Other streets in the tract have literary allusions
Originally ran just a couple blocks; extended by city ordinance in 1911

streets.who: From old Rancho La Brea with famous tar deposits
facts: Tar first coated native canoes, then adobe roofs
Rancho deeded in 1828 to Rocha family, who lost it to their own lawyer Henry Hancock in legal fees
Hancock's son George discovered oil, developed Hancock Park
Originally Plummer Street; renamed La Brea in 1906

streets.who: Vicente Feliz (c. 1741-1822), corporal who protected LA's first 44 non-native settlers
facts: Spanish crown granted Vicente 6,647 acres: Rancho Los Feliz, around 1800
Los Feliz means 'the Feliz family,' not 'the happy'
Pronunciation debate: old-timers say 'loss FEE-liz,' transplants say 'lohs fuh-LEASE'
Widened from avenue to boulevard in 1912

streets.namedAfter: Being the main street — from Calle Principal
streets.who: Likely the oldest street in LA
facts: Began as one of two anonymous roads from the original pueblo's plaza in 1781
Known as Calle Real, then Calle Principal, then Main Street
Some historians believe surveyor Ord confused Main with what is now Los Angeles Street on his 1849 map

streets.who: Built the LA Aqueduct; career ended by St. Francis Dam disaster
facts: Born in Belfast, worked from ditch cleaner to head of Water Department
Famous quote at aqueduct opening: 'There it is. Take it.'
St. Francis Dam failed March 12, 1928, killing over 450 people
Mulholland Drive laid out in 1922 when things were still going well

streets.namedAfter: Unknown French inspiration — possibly a steamer or hotel
streets.who: Renamed by developer Edwin S. Rowley from Oshkosh, Wisconsin
facts: Originally Rosedale Avenue because it led to Rosedale Cemetery
Rowley didn't want to sell houses on a street named after a graveyard
Possibly inspired by transatlantic steamer La Normandie or a DC hotel

streets.who: Helped negotiate Treaty of Cahuenga; LA County's first superior court judge
facts: Not actually the oldest street in LA despite appearances
Previously called Wine Street or Vine Street, or 'the street next to Judge Olvera's house'
Christine Sterling transformed it into a Mexican-themed marketplace in 1930 with help from LA Times publisher Harry Chandler and prison labor

streets.namedAfter: Lt. Edward Otho Cresap Ord (1818-1883), U.S. Army engineer
streets.who: Created the first American map of Los Angeles in 1849
facts: Roomed with William Tecumseh Sherman at West Point
Later forced Robert E. Lee to surrender
Originally Calle Alta ('High Street'), renamed Walters Street in 1886, then Ord in 1890
Most streets on his 1849 map didn't yet exist — the work was aspirational

streets.namedAfter: Pio de Jesus Pico (1801-1894), last Mexican governor of Alta California
streets.who: Born at Mission San Gabriel; of Spanish, Native American, and African heritage
facts: LA never had an unlucky 13th Street — the street after 12th was dubbed Pico Street
Financed luxury hotel Pico House (still standing) by selling San Fernando Valley land
Gambling debts left him penniless
Became Pico Boulevard in 1914

streets.who: German Jewish immigrant who crossed paths with Abraham Lincoln in Illinois
facts: Not named for a color or metal, but for a man
Elected City Council president in 1896
Silver Lake Reservoir named in his honor in 1903 when Mulholland announced plans to dam Ivanhoe Canyon

streets.who: Pio Pico's niece — surveyor Ord allegedly found her charming
facts: Surveyor Edward Ord allegedly named it after Trinidad Ortega from San Diego
May have previously been Calle de Caridad (Charity Street) or Calle Cuidado (Caution Street)
The romance angle is 'surely hogwash' per the site, but the Ortega connection was promoted at a 1932 luncheon by Trinidad's daughter

streets.namedAfter: A planned town called 'Sunset' (1887) that never materialized
streets.who: Boulevard was the main drag of a ghost town that never existed
facts: Planned town 'Sunset' announced in present-day Westwood in June 1887 — but never materialized
Started small, absorbed streets named Bread, Short, Elysian, and Marchessault
The Sunset Strip in WeHo was known as Santa Monica Avenue
Easternmost portion renamed Cesar Chavez Avenue in 1994

streets.who: Opened LA's first general store c. 1828; created modern DTLA with his Temple Block buildings
facts: One of LA's earliest American settlers, became a Mexican citizen ('Juan')
Owned Rancho Los Cerritos (today's Long Beach)
His half-brother Francis Pliny Fisk Temple was another major land-grabber

streets.namedAfter: Vermont (the state), via Ozro W. Childs (1824-1890)
streets.who: LA's most famous Vermonter: banker, developer, horticulturalist
facts: Originally Childs Street on the 1876 West Los Angeles tract near USC
Childs and partners John G. Downey and Isaias W. Hellman donated this tract for the founding of USC in 1879
Vermont Avenue named by 1882, almost certainly after Childs's home state

streets.who: Former U.S. Senator (R-CA) who planted a vineyard
facts: Originally Weyse Avenue (1887) after Otto Gunther Weyse, Harvey Wilcox's partner
Weyse was declared insolvent in 1888, 'accidentally' shot himself in 1889, went through an abuse-filled divorce in 1890, and died at 34
Cole named Vine Street after his grapevines in 1893; the name crept north and replaced Weyse Avenue

streets.who: Genuinely was west of LA city limits when opened
facts: Opened in 1885 as a county road, genuinely west of LA city limits
Hoover Street marked LA's western boundary at the time
City expanded to meet Western Avenue in 1896
Now about 27.5 miles long

streets.who: Cincinnati banker's son who went by 'Gaylord'; befriended Jack London and George Bernard Shaw
facts: Bought a barley field in 1887, failed to find oil, ran a boulevard through it in 1895
Named it after himself
Final years spent hawking a quack device called 'Ionaco'
Route follows a path used for eons — by indigenous peoples and even Ice Age mammals heading to the La Brea Tar Pits
